title: Public Space and Private Screens: The Commute Is Not Empty
thesis: When shared physical spaces become collections of private screens, societies lose low-cost channels for knowledge exchange, solidarity, and cross-class encounter that no app can fully replace.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: Mass-media participation is nearly universal in India, while voluntary social participation has fallen sharply.

evidence.snippet: The NSSO Time Use Survey 2024 finds that 93% of Indians aged 6 and above participate in mass media or leisure activities, while voluntary social participation has declined.
Putnam documents the decline of in-person civic engagement and voluntary association in modern societies.

counterevidence.summary: The NSSO survey measures participation categories, not platform-specific attention or the quality of social interaction; some digital participation may also be social.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

claims.claim: Public transit and waiting spaces in India show high rates of individual screen use across income groups.

evidence.snippet: Rising leisure time and near-universal mass-media participation suggest large amounts of screen use occur in public and transit contexts.
A Pew study found that 23% of cellphone owners in the U.S. used their phones in public to avoid interacting with others, illustrating how devices reshape public behavior.

counterevidence.summary: There is no nationally representative India survey of transit or public-space screen use; the claim rests on observational pattern and inference rather than census data.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

claims.claim: Incidental conversation in public spaces historically served as a low-cost channel for information, apprenticeship, and social cohesion.

evidence.snippet: Oldenburg argues that third places — informal public gathering spots — are anchors of community life and facilitate creative interaction across social boundaries.
Putnam links routine in-person association to the formation of social capital, trust, and civic engagement.
The NSSO Time Use Survey 2024 shows near-universal mass-media participation alongside declining voluntary social participation, implying an opportunity cost for incidental public interaction.

counterevidence.summary: Not all incidental public interaction is positive; harassment, discrimination, and unwanted contact can make withdrawal a rational and protective choice.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

claims.claim: Headphones and private feeds convert shared space into parallel solitude, making social withdrawal feel like a norm.

evidence.snippet: Pew found that a significant share of cellphone owners use phones in public to avoid interaction, normalizing device-mediated withdrawal.
Turkle describes 'alone together' — physical proximity combined with psychological distance created by always-on personal devices.

counterevidence.summary: Headphones and feeds can also create personal safety, autonomy, and restorative solitude in crowded or stressful environments; withdrawal is not always a loss.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

sources.title: Ray Oldenburg: The Great Good Place
Pew Research Center: Americans' Views on Mobile Etiquette

agentInstructions: Treat the article as observational and structural, not as a strict causal claim about phones causing civic decline.
Use claim IDs as the retrieval unit and route quantitative or mental-health questions to the appropriate Diagnosis articles.
Preserve the evidence-cautious tone; acknowledge that digital connection and personal safety are valid reasons for withdrawal.
When citing sources, use the source IDs and URLs from the artifact; do not invent India-specific transit screen-time data.
Refer to related articles by title and canonical path, not by internal arc or lane labels.
A before-and-after Mermaid diagram comparing a shared train compartment in 2005 and 2025 is embedded after Claim C2; preserve it when editing the article.

reviews.notes: Re-approved by maintainer after the meta#61 P2 series migration (hardcoded kicker strip + arc reorder; no prose change beyond the kicker line; issue #124 instruction).
